region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent (South Asia); widely consumed in Singapore's Indian community

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for barfi; mapped to the nearest parent class 'confection food product' (FOODON_03301710). A more specific barfi entry does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sugar confectionery — closest FoodEx2 category for barfi as a dense sugar-fat confection made from gram flour, sugar, and ghee.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a barfi-specific code. A0CKY (sugar confectionery) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for barfi as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of barfi weighs approximately 30 g, which is a typical single-serving portion as served at Indian sweet shops or festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Barfi — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Barfi (cr_id F13010001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from the known fat composition of ghee and gram flour; detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of gram flour (besan) and sugar-based Indian confections — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for gram flour products and sugar confections
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for barfi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated based on: (1) gram flour (besan) has a GI of approximately 28–35 (low), but (2) the dominant carbohydrate fraction in barfi is refined sugar (44g/100g of 55g/100g total carbohydrate), which has a GI of approximately 65–68. The blended GI is estimated at 65 (medium), weighted toward the sugar fraction. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 16.5g) / 100 = 10.7. Serving size used: 30g piece, providing approximately 16.5g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (44g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.5g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; high saturated fat (~19.5g/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness and endothelial dysfunction.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid barfi or strictly limit intake to one very small piece (15–20g) on rare festive occasions, as its combination of high refined sugar and high saturated fat is particularly unfavourable for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 44g/100g far exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling; total carbohydrate is 55g/100g with only 1.5g/100g dietary fibre, producing a high glycaemic load and rapid postprandial glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Barfi is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be avoided entirely; if consumed on rare festive occasions, limit to one very small piece (10–15g) and monitor blood glucose closely, as the high refined sugar and saturated fat combination worsens both glycaemic control and insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~19.5g/100g from ghee) raises LDL cholesterol; trans fat (~0.2g/100g) further increases cardiovascular risk; very high sugar (44g/100g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid barfi due to its high saturated fat, trans fat, and refined sugar content, all of which adversely affect LDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and endothelial function; if consumed, limit to one very small piece (10–15g) on rare occasions only.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (~19.5g/100g from ghee), trans fat (~0.2g/100g), and extremely high refined sugar (44g/100g) are all strongly pro-inflammatory and exacerbate RA joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid barfi entirely, as its combination of high saturated fat from ghee, trans fat, and refined sugar promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines relevant to RA pathophysiology.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Barfi (also spelled Burfi or Burfee) is a traditional Indian sweet confection originating from the Indian subcontinent and widely consumed across South Asian communities in Singapore and globally. It is made by cooking gram flour (besan) with sugar syrup, ghee (clarified butter), and aromatic spices such as cardamom, then setting the mixture into a dense, fudge-like slab that is cut into squares or diamond shapes and garnished with pistachios and almonds. The texture is firm yet melt-in-the-mouth, with a rich, sweet, and mildly nutty flavour profile. Barfi is a calorie-dense food providing 541 kcal per 100g, with a high fat content of 33.4g (predominantly saturated fat from ghee at approximately 19.5g/100g) and a very high sugar content of 44g/100g out of 55g/100g total carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is low at 1.5g/100g. Sodium is low at 12mg/100g. Calcium is 31mg/100g and iron is 2mg/100g. Clinically, barfi is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes (sugar far exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling), hypertension (high sugar and saturated fat combination), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat and trans fat raise LDL and triglycerides), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory fat and sugar profile). Barfi is commonly served at Indian festivals such as Diwali, Eid, and weddings, and is available at Indian sweet shops (mithai shops) in Singapore's Little India district. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan due to ghee content.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of barfi?
What is the difference between barfi and burfi?
How much sugar is in barfi?
Is barfi good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F13010001) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from known ghee composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for gram flour and sugar fractions, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names are present.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this barfi preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F13010001) as of 2026. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry and are not available from ICMR-NIN for this specific gram flour barfi formulation as of 2026. trans_fat_g of 0.2g/100g is an estimate based on known ghee trans fat content (primarily vaccenic acid and conjugated linoleic acid); this value is not directly reported in the HPB entry. saturated_fat_g of 19.5g/100g is estimated from the known fatty acid profile of ghee (approximately 60–65% saturated fat of total fat) applied to the reported total fat of 33.4g/100g; this value is not directly reported in the HPB entry. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for barfi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The description in the input JSON references gram flour as the base ingredient, which is consistent with besan barfi; khoya (milk solids) is referenced in health suitability notes but is not listed in the input description — this record reflects the gram flour formulation as described in the HPB source.
